presenting_problem.what_brings_you_to_my_office_briefly_des:
Over the past year, I’ve been feeling increasingly overwhelmed, anxious, and down, to the point that it’s starting to affect my work, my marriage, and how I show up as a dad. I work full-time as a project manager for a mid-sized software company, and what used to feel like normal job stress now feels unmanageable. I have trouble focusing in meetings, my mind jumps to worst-case scenarios, and I’m constantly worried I’m going to drop the ball or get fired, even though my performance reviews are technically fine. 

At home, my wife has commented that I seem distant and irritable. I snap at small things, especially with my kids (ages 7 and 4), like when they’re loud or don’t listen the first time. Afterwards I feel guilty and ashamed, and I often end up withdrawing—going to the bedroom, scrolling on my phone, or zoning out in front of the TV—rather than engaging with my family. I’ve also noticed that I’m drinking more than I used to. I used to have a beer or two on weekends, but now I’m having 2–3 drinks most nights to “wind down,” and I’m starting to worry that I’m using alcohol to cope instead of actually dealing with what’s going on.

Physically, I feel tired all the time even when I sleep 7–8 hours. I wake up with a sense of dread about the day ahead. I’ve had several episodes that I think might be panic attacks: sudden racing heart, tightness in my chest, feeling like I can’t catch my breath, and this overwhelming sense that something terrible is about to happen. The first time it happened I actually went to urgent care because I thought I might be having a heart attack; they ran tests and said everything looked normal and suggested it was anxiety.

Emotionally, I’m more down and hopeless than I’ve ever been. I wouldn’t say I’m actively suicidal, but I do have thoughts like, “If I just didn’t wake up tomorrow, maybe that would be easier,” or “My family might be better off without me messing things up.” Those thoughts scare me, and I don’t really talk about them with anyone. I also feel like I’ve lost interest in things I used to enjoy, like playing guitar, going hiking, and meeting up with friends. I make excuses to avoid social plans because I feel drained and don’t want to pretend everything is okay.

What finally pushed me to seek help was a recent argument with my wife where she said she feels like she’s “losing” me and that she’s worried about me. She gently suggested therapy a few months ago, and I brushed it off, but after this last argument and another panic-like episode at work, I realized I can’t keep trying to white-knuckle my way through this. I want to understand what’s going on with me, learn healthier ways to cope, and hopefully get back to feeling more like myself—for my own sake and for my family.
presenting_problem.how_long_has_this_been_occurring: Looking back, I think some of these issues have been brewing for a few years, but they became much more noticeable and disruptive about 10–12 months ago. The constant, almost daily anxiety really ramped up around last winter, and the more intense panic-like episodes started about 6 months ago. The increase in drinking and the more frequent irritability with my family have been going on for roughly the last 4–6 months. The low mood and loss of interest in things I used to enjoy have been pretty consistent for about the past year, but in the last 3 months it’s felt significantly worse—like it’s not just a rough patch anymore but something I can’t shake on my own.
presenting_problem.what_do_you_believe_caused_it:
I think it’s a combination of several factors building up over time rather than one single event. Professionally, my job has gotten more demanding over the last two years. Our company went through a reorganization, and I ended up managing more projects and a larger team without a clear increase in support or resources. I tend to put a lot of pressure on myself to not disappoint anyone, so I’ve been saying yes to too many things and working late nights just to keep up. I also work remotely most of the time, so my work and home boundaries have blurred—I catch myself checking email late at night or on weekends, which makes it feel like I’m never really off.

On the personal side, becoming a parent of two young kids and trying to be a good husband and provider has been both rewarding and very stressful. Our younger child still wakes up some nights, and we’ve had several rounds of illness (colds, ear infections, etc.) that disrupt sleep and routines. My wife and I don’t have a lot of extended family nearby, so we don’t get much regular help or childcare breaks. I think the chronic sleep disruption and constant feeling of being “on” as a parent have worn me down more than I realized.

There are also some deeper, long-standing patterns I’m starting to recognize. I grew up in a family where emotions weren’t really talked about—my dad especially believed in just “toughing it out,” and there was a lot of criticism when I made mistakes. I learned early on to keep my feelings to myself and to measure my worth by how productive or useful I am. I suspect that those beliefs are feeding into my current anxiety and perfectionism, and that I never really learned healthy ways to cope with stress.

The pandemic years probably played a role as well. Even though things are more open now, those years of isolation, working from home, and worrying about health and finances seemed to plant a baseline level of anxiety in me that never fully went away. I also noticed my social circle shrank during that time, and I never really rebuilt it, so I don’t have many people I feel comfortable opening up to.

Finally, I think I’ve been ignoring warning signs for a long time—using distractions like my phone, TV, and alcohol to avoid uncomfortable feelings instead of dealing with them. That avoidance might have worked in the short term, but I think it’s caught up with me, and now everything feels like too much at once.
presenting_problem.what_have_you_tried_to_change_it:
I’ve tried a mix of self-help approaches and small lifestyle changes, but nothing has really stuck or made a big difference. Over the last year, I’ve:

- **Exercise and routine:** I tried getting back into running, aiming for 3 times a week. For a few weeks at a time I’d manage 20–30 minute runs in the neighborhood, and I did feel a bit better on the days I exercised. But when work got busy or the kids got sick, I’d fall off the routine and then feel discouraged and guilty for not keeping it up.

- **Sleep and screen time:** I’ve made some attempts at better sleep hygiene—like setting a goal to be in bed by 11:00 p.m., keeping my phone out of the bedroom, and not checking work email after 9:00 p.m. Sometimes I manage this for a week or so, but then I slide back into scrolling on my phone in bed or staying up late watching shows because it’s the only time I feel like I have to myself. My sleep is still pretty restless, and I often wake up around 3–4 a.m. with my mind racing.

- **Cutting back on alcohol:** I’ve told myself multiple times that I’m going to limit drinking to weekends only or to one drink a night. I’ll stick to it for a short period, but then after a stressful day, I’ll justify having “just one more” to take the edge off. I haven’t had any major negative consequences from drinking, but I don’t like that I’m relying on it as a coping mechanism.

- **Self-help resources:** I’ve listened to a few mental health podcasts and read articles online about anxiety, burnout, and depression. I even bought a workbook on cognitive behavioral techniques for anxiety. I started a few exercises—like writing down anxious thoughts and trying to challenge them—but I struggled to stay consistent without guidance and wasn’t sure if I was doing it “right.”

- **Talking to my wife and friends:** I’ve tried opening up a little bit to my wife about feeling overwhelmed and anxious, but I tend to downplay it or make jokes so it doesn’t turn into a big, serious conversation. I did mention to a close friend that I thought I might be burned out, and he suggested therapy and offered to check in with me more often, but I haven’t really followed up. I still find it uncomfortable to admit how bad it sometimes feels.

- **Medical checkup:** I went to urgent care once when I thought I might be having a heart issue, and they told me it was likely anxiety. After that, I scheduled a physical with my primary care doctor. He ran some basic labs, which came back normal, and suggested I consider therapy and possibly medication if things didn’t improve. At the time, I wasn’t ready to start medication and told myself I could handle it on my own.

Overall, I’ve made several attempts to fix things by myself in bits and pieces, but I’ve realized I need more structured support, accountability, and professional guidance. That’s why I’m finally reaching out for therapy now instead of continuing to try to patch things together on my own.

household_and_relationships.who_lives_in_your_home_with_you:
I live in a two-bedroom apartment with my wife and our two children.

- **Spouse:** Emma (age 37) – my wife of 9 years. She works part-time as a nurse at a local clinic and is usually home in the afternoons and evenings. We share parenting responsibilities and household tasks.
- **Child 1:** Lucas (age 7) – our son. He’s in second grade at the local elementary school. He’s energetic, loves soccer and Legos, and tends to be pretty sensitive to conflict between me and my wife.
- **Child 2:** Maya (age 4) – our daughter. She attends a part-time preschool program. She’s very attached to both of us, especially my wife, and still occasionally wakes up at night.

We do not have any other relatives or roommates living with us. We have a cat named Olive (3 years old), but I’m not sure if that’s relevant—I’m including it because she’s very much part of our household routine and sometimes even her needs (like vet visits or when she’s sick) add to the sense of having a lot on my plate.
household_and_relationships.how_would_you_describe_your_current_rela:
Overall, I would describe our relationship as loving and committed, but currently strained and under a lot of stress. Emma and I have been together for about 12 years and married for 9. We genuinely care about each other and share similar values about family, honesty, and raising our kids. We still have moments where we laugh together, watch shows, or talk about our days, and we both say that we’re in this for the long haul.

That said, the past couple of years—especially since our second child was born and with increasing work and financial stress—have taken a toll. We argue more frequently about household responsibilities, parenting styles, and how much time I spend working or on my phone. Emma has told me she feels like she’s carrying more of the emotional and mental load for the family, and that I sometimes “check out” when I’m stressed instead of talking to her. From my side, I often feel criticized or like I can’t do anything quite right, which makes me defensive and more likely to shut down.

Our communication has become more surface-level at times. We coordinate logistics (kids’ schedules, groceries, bills) but don’t always make space to talk about how we’re really feeling. When we do try to have deeper conversations—especially about my anxiety or her feeling overwhelmed—they sometimes turn into arguments or I get overwhelmed and withdraw. Intimacy (both emotional and physical) has decreased over the last year, partly due to exhaustion, my low mood, and her feeling disconnected from me.

Despite the challenges, we both have expressed that we want to work on things. She has encouraged me to seek therapy, not in a blaming way but because she’s genuinely worried about me. I think we’re at a point where we could really benefit from me getting individual support, and possibly couples therapy down the line, so that our relationship doesn’t deteriorate further.
